Also check if there are other callers of `read_file_from_ref` and list them.
FIRST check docs/learned/index.md for existing documentation on this topic. Read relevant docs before exploring raw files. Then explore codebase as necessary.

1→<!-- AUTO-GENERATED FILE - DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY --> 2→<!-- Edit source frontmatter, then run 'erk docs sync' to regenerate. --> 3→ 4→# Agent Documentation 5→ 6→Before starting work, scan the read-when conditions below. 7→If your current task matches, read the linked document **before writing code**. 8→ 9→## Categories 10→ 11→- [architecture/](architecture/) — Explore when working on core patterns (dry-run, gateways, subprocess, shell integration). Add docs here for cross-cutting technical patterns. 12→- [capabilities/](capabilities/) 13→- [changelog/](changelog/) 14→- [checklists/](checklists/) 15→- [ci/](ci/) 16→- [claude-code/](claude-code/) 17→- [cli/](cli/) — Explore when building CLI commands or output formatting. Add docs here for Click patterns and terminal UX. 18→- [commands/](commands/) — Explore when creating or optimizing slash commands. Add docs here for command authoring patterns. 19→- [config/](config/) 20→- [configuration/](configuration/) 21→- [documentation/](documentation/) — Explore when structuring or writing documentation. Add docs here for documentation methodology and best practices. 22→- [erk/](erk/) — Explore when working with erk-specific workflows (worktrees, PR sync, Graphite). Add docs here for erk user-facing features. 23→- [erk-dev/](erk-dev/) 24→- [gateway/](gateway/) 25→- [hooks/](hooks/) — Explore when creating or debugging hooks. Add docs here for hook development patterns. 26→- [integrations/](integrations/) 27→- [objectives/](objectives/) 28→- [planning/](planning/) — Explore when working with plans, .erk/impl-context/ folders, or agent delegation. Add docs here for planning workflow patterns. 29→- [pr-operations/](pr-operations/) 30→- [refactoring/](refactoring/)

session.assistant: Now let me check the architecture documentation for gateway patterns:

session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: Mixed Exception/Union Pattern (Deprecated) 3→last_audited: "2026-02-07 21:45 PT" 4→audit_result: edited 5→read_when: 6→ - "considering mixing discriminated unions with exceptions in gateway methods" 7→ - "designing operations with both expected failures and cleanup steps" 8→ - "reviewing historical gateway error handling experiments" 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: "mixing discriminated unions with exception-based cleanup in a single method" 11→ warning: "This pattern was tried in PR #6347 and reverted in PR #6375. Message-only discriminated unions with no domain-meaningful variants add complexity without value. Use exceptions for all error cases, or use discriminated unions with meaningful variants throughout. Document why in the PR." 12→--- 13→ 14→# Mixed Exception/Union Pattern (Deprecated) 15→ 16→**Status**: This pattern was implemented and then reverted. It is **not currently used** in erk. 17→ 18→This document explains WHY the mixed exception/union pattern was tried and WHY it was abandoned, to prevent future attempts at the same failed approach. 19→ 20→## The Attempted Pattern 21→ 22→In January 2026, we experimented with mixing error handling strategies in a single gateway method: 23→ 24→- **Main operation** returns discriminated union (`WorktreeRemoved | WorktreeRemoveError`) 25→- **Cleanup operation** raises exception (`RuntimeError`) 26→ 27→The hypothesis was that this distinguished between "expected failures" (main operation) and "corrupted state" (cleanup failures). 28→ 29→## Why It Was Tried 30→
... [92 lines omitted] ...
33→The `remove_worktree` method had two distinct failure modes: 35→1. **Expected failures**: Worktree doesn't exist, is in use, permission denied → could be handled gracefully 36→2. **Cleanup failures**: `git worktree prune` fails → indicates repository corruption 38→The discriminated union conversion aimed to make expected failures explicit while keeping cleanup failures exceptional. 44→<!-- Source: PR #6375 - Convert Worktree add/remove back to exceptions --> 50→The discriminated union types (`WorktreeRemoved`, `WorktreeRemoveError`) were **structureless message wrappers**: 54→- Callers couldn't distinguish between error types anyway 59→### 2. Exception Boundaries Already Exist 61→The CLI commands already catch exceptions at natural boundaries (Click command handlers). Moving the try/except from CLI to gateway didn't eliminate exception handling - it just moved it. 63→**The lesson**: Exception-based error handling works fine when exceptions are caught at architectural boundaries (CLI layer). Don't convert to discriminated unions just to avoid exceptions. 67→Having one method return a union for some errors and raise exceptions for others creates cognitive load: 69→- Callers must remember which errors are in the return type and which are exceptions 80→Gateway methods use **exceptions for all failures**. See `remove_worktree` in `packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/git/worktree/abc.py` — it returns `None` and raises `RuntimeError` on failure. No discriminated union types involved. 82→CLI commands catch these exceptions at their natural boundaries (Click command handlers), converting them to `click.ClickException` for user-facing output. See callers in `src/erk/cli/commands/wt/delete_cmd.py` and `src/erk/cli/commands/navigation_helpers.py`. 84→This is simpler, less code, and works well when all callers terminate identically on failure — no caller branches on error content or inspects error structure. 97→See `docs/learned/architecture/discriminated-union-error-handling.md` for current discriminated union patterns. 101→When designing gateway error handling: 105→| Single failure mode with message | Exception | No variants to distinguish | 106→| Multiple failure modes, callers don't distinguish | Exception | Type system overhead without benefit | 107→| Multiple failure modes, callers DO distinguish | Discriminated union | Type narrowing adds value | 110→**Never mix exceptions and discriminated unions in a single method signature.** Choose one strategy for all error cases. 114→- [Discriminated Union Error Handling](discriminated-union-error-handling.md) - When and how to use discriminated unions

1→--- 2→title: Subprocess Wrappers 3→last_audited: "2026-02-16 00:00 PT" 4→audit_result: edited 5→read_when: 6→ - "using subprocess wrappers" 7→ - "executing shell commands" 8→ - "understanding subprocess patterns" 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: "using bare subprocess.run with check=True" 11→ warning: "Use wrapper functions: run_subprocess_with_context() (gateway) or run_with_error_reporting() (CLI). Exception: Graceful degradation pattern with explicit CalledProcessError handling is acceptable for optional operations." 12→ pattern: "subprocess\\.run\\(" 13→ - action: "adding a Claude subprocess call with --print mode" 14→ warning: "Always include --no-session-persistence flag and use env=build_claude_subprocess_env() parameter. Both are required to prevent session persistence and CLAUDECODE context leakage. See the 'Claude Subprocess Environment' section." 15→ - action: "using sed -i in scripts that run on both macOS and Linux" 16→ warning: "macOS sed requires `sed -i ''` (empty string argument) while Linux sed uses `sed -i` (no argument). Scripts that use sed -i without handling this difference will fail silently on one platform." 17→ score: 4 18→--- 19→ 20→# Subprocess Execution Wrappers 21→ 22→**NEVER use bare `subprocess.run(..., check=True)`. ALWAYS use wrapper functions.** 23→ 24→This guide explains the two-layer pattern for subprocess execution in erk: gateway layer and CLI layer wrappers. 25→ 26→## Scope 27→ 28→**These rules apply to production erk code** in `src/erk/` and `packages/erk-shared/`. 29→ 30→**Exception: erk-dev** (`packages/erk-dev/`) is developer tooling and is exempt from these rules. Direct `subprocess.run` is acceptable in erk-dev commands since they don't need the testability/dry-run benefits of wrapper functions.
... [348 lines omitted] ...
34→Erk uses a two-layer design for subprocess execution to provide consistent error handling across different boundaries: 36→- **Gateway layer**: `run_subprocess_with_context()` - Raises RuntimeError for business logic 37→- **CLI layer**: `run_with_error_reporting()` - Prints user-friendly message and raises SystemExit 47→**Behavior**: Raises `RuntimeError` with rich context on failure 49→**Usage**: Pass `cmd` (the command list), `operation_context` (human-readable description), and `cwd` (working directory). On failure, raises `RuntimeError` with the operation context, command, exit code, and stderr. 57→- **Rich error messages**: Includes operation context, command, exit code, stderr 58→- **Exception chaining**: Preserves original CalledProcessError for debugging 61→### run_with_error_reporting (CLI Layer) 63→**When to use**: In CLI command handlers where you want to immediately exit on failure with a user-friendly message. 65→**Import**: `from erk.cli.subprocess_utils import run_with_error_reporting` 67→**Behavior**: Prints error message to stderr and raises `SystemExit` on failure 72→from erk.cli.subprocess_utils import run_with_error_reporting 74→# ✅ CORRECT: User-friendly error messages + SystemExit 75→run_with_error_reporting( 84→- **User-friendly**: Error messages are clear and actionable 86→- **No exception handling needed**: Wrapper handles everything 90→- **Rich error messages**: Both wrappers include operation context, command, exit code, and stderr 91→- **Exception chaining**: Preserves original CalledProcessError for debugging 92→- **Consistent patterns**: Two clear boundaries with appropriate error handling 93→- **Debugging support**: Full context available in error messages and logs 110→Not all subprocess calls should use `run_with_error_reporting()`. Use explicit exception handling when: 112→1. **The operation is optional** - Failure should not stop the main workflow 114→3. **Warning vs Error** - You want to show a warning and continue, not exit 119→# Pattern: check=True with explicit CalledProcessError handling 123→except subprocess.CalledProcessError as e: 124→ # Show warning, continue execution 125→ user_output(click.style("⚠ ", fg="yellow") + f"Optional operation failed: {e}") 126→except FileNotFoundError: 135→| CLI command that must succeed | `run_with_error_reporting()` | SystemExit on failure is correct | 136→| Optional background operation | Explicit exception handling | Main operation should continue | 137→| Gateway real.py implementation | `run_subprocess_with_context()` | Consistent error wrapping | 141→For GitHub API commands that may fail due to transient network errors, use `execute_gh_command_with_retry()`: 151→- Automatic retry on transient errors (network timeouts, connection failures) 157→## Error Accumulation Pattern 183→# Use accumulated stderr in error msg 190→- Stderr is accumulated as list, joined only when needed for error message 259→| **Error handling** | Returns `None` on any failure | Raises exception or returns error type | 262→| **Caller responsibility** | Check for `None`, decide how to handle | Catch exception or check error type | 270→See `_get_pr_for_plan_direct()` in `src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/trigger_async_learn.py`. Returns `dict[str, object] | None` — `None` on any failure (missing issue, metadata, branch, or PR). 274→- **No exceptions** - Never raises, always returns `None` on failure 275→- **No error messages** - Caller decides what to log 276→- **Uniform failure** - All failure modes return `None` consistently 286→Use when the operation is **critical** and failure should be explicit: 288→See `get_pr_for_plan()` in `src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/get_pr_for_plan.py` for the full implementation. It is a Click command that raises `SystemExit(1)` with JSON error output on any failure, and attempts branch_name recovery via pattern matching. 292→- **Explicit errors** - Each failure mode has specific error message 294→- **Clear contract** - Caller knows exceptions mean critical failure 298→- User-facing commands where failure needs explanation 300→- CLI commands that should exit with error message 331→The same PR lookup in `get_pr_for_plan.py` is **strict** because it's a user-facing command where the user explicitly asked for the PR and expects either the answer or a clear error message. 367→- **CLI layer**: Use `run_with_error_reporting()` for command handlers

1→"""Abstract base class for Git commit operations. 2→ 3→This sub-gateway extracts commit operations from the main Git gateway, 4→including staging, committing, amending, and commit message queries. 5→""" 6→ 7→from __future__ import annotations 8→ 9→from abc import ABC, abstractmethod 10→from pathlib import Path 11→ 12→ 13→class GitCommitOps(ABC): 14→ """Abstract interface for Git commit operations. 15→ 16→ This interface contains both mutation and query operations for commits. 17→ All implementations (real, fake, dry-run, printing) must implement this interface. 18→ """ 19→ 20→ # ============================================================================ 21→ # Mutation Operations 22→ # ============================================================================ 23→ 24→ @abstractmethod 25→ def stage_files(self, cwd: Path, paths: list[str], *, force: bool) -> None: 26→ """Stage specific files for commit. 27→ 28→ Args: 29→ cwd: Working directory 30→ paths: List of file paths to stage (relative to cwd)

session.assistant: Now let me look at the real implementation to understand the pattern:
session.tool_result:
1→"""Production implementation of Git commit operations using subprocess.""" 2→ 3→import os 4→import subprocess 5→import tempfile 6→from pathlib import Path 7→ 8→from erk_shared.gateway.git.commit_ops.abc import GitCommitOps 9→from erk_shared.gateway.git.lock import wait_for_index_lock 10→from erk_shared.gateway.time.abc import Time 11→from erk_shared.subprocess_utils import run_subprocess_with_context 12→ 13→ 14→class RealGitCommitOps(GitCommitOps): 15→ """Real implementation of Git commit operations using subprocess.""" 16→ 17→ def __init__(self, time: Time) -> None: 18→ """Initialize RealGitCommitOps with Time provider. 19→ 20→ Args: 21→ time: Time provider for lock waiting 22→ """ 23→ self._time = time 24→ 25→ # ============================================================================ 26→ # Mutation Operations 27→ # ============================================================================ 28→ 29→ def stage_files(self, cwd: Path, paths: list[str], *, force: bool) -> None: 30→ """Stage specific files for commit."""
... [55 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant: Let me get the read_file_from_ref implementation:

1→"""Push a preprocessed session to the async-learn branch with accumulation. 2→ 3→Preprocesses session JSONL to compressed XML and accumulates multiple sessions 4→on the same branch across lifecycle stages. 5→ 6→Usage: 7→ erk exec push-session \\ 8→ --session-file /path/to/session.jsonl \\ 9→ --session-id abc-123 \\ 10→ --stage planning \\ 11→ --source local \\ 12→ --plan-id 2521 13→ 14→Output: 15→ Structured JSON output with upload status: 16→ {"uploaded": true, "plan_id": 2521, "session_branch": "async-learn/2521", "files": [...]} 17→ {"uploaded": false, "reason": "preprocessing_failed"} 18→ 19→Exit Codes: 20→ 0: Always (non-critical operation, graceful degradation) 21→""" 22→ 23→import json 24→import subprocess 25→import tempfile 26→from datetime import UTC 27→from pathlib import Path 28→from typing import Literal 29→ 30→import click

1→"""Fetch preprocessed sessions from an async-learn branch. 2→ 3→Downloads the manifest and XML files from the async-learn/{plan_id} branch 4→for use by the learn pipeline. Returns JSON with file list and manifest metadata. 5→ 6→Usage: 7→ erk exec fetch-sessions --plan-id 2521 --output-dir ./learn 8→ 9→Output: 10→ Structured JSON output: 11→ {"success": true, "plan_id": 2521, "manifest": {...}, "files": [...]} 12→ {"success": false, "error": "branch_not_found"} 13→ 14→Exit Codes: 15→ 0: Success 16→ 1: Error (branch not found, fetch failed) 17→""" 18→ 19→import json 20→from pathlib import Path 21→ 22→import click 23→ 24→from erk_shared.context.helpers import require_git, require_repo_root 25→from erk_shared.gateway.git.abc import Git 26→ 27→ 28→def _fetch_manifest( 29→ *, 30→ repo_root: Path,

session.assistant:
Excellent! Now I have all the information I need. Let me create a comprehensive report:
## Report: `read_file_from_ref` Gateway Pattern Analysis
### Key Location in download_remote_session.py
**File**: `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/download_remote_session.py`
**Lines 77-81** - Gateway call pattern:
```python content = git.commit.read_file_from_ref( repo_root, ref=f"origin/{session_branch}", file_path=f".erk/session/{session_id}.jsonl", ) ```
**Pattern**: This replaces direct subprocess with a gateway abstraction. The gateway method encapsulates the `git show` operation.
### Gateway Implementation
**Abstract definition**: `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/git/commit_ops/abc.py` (lines 152-163)
```python @abstractmethod def read_file_from_ref(self, cwd: Path, *, ref: str, file_path: str) -> bytes | None: """Read file content from a git reference without checkout.
Args: cwd: Repository root directory. ref: Git reference (branch, tag, SHA, e.g. "origin/async-learn/42"). file_path: Path relative to repository root.
Returns: File content as bytes, or None if ref or file doesn't exist. """ ... ```
**Real implementation**: `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/git/commit_ops/real.py` (lines 147-157)
```python def read_file_from_ref(self, cwd: Path, *, ref: str, file_path: str) -> bytes | None: """Read file content from a git reference without checkout.""" result = subprocess.run( ["git", "show", f"{ref}:{file_path}"], cwd=cwd, capture_output=True, check=False, ) if result.returncode != 0: return None return result.stdout ```
**Pattern note**: This implementation uses `subprocess.run()` with `check=False` and explicit return code checking. This is an intentional LBYL (Look Before You Leap) pattern for graceful degradation - when the file doesn't exist at the ref, it returns `None` rather than raising an exception.
### All Callers of read_file_from_ref
**1. download_remote_session.py** (lines 77-81) - Location: `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/download_remote_session.py` - Usage: Downloads a session JSONL from a remote branch - File path: `.erk/session/{session_id}.jsonl`
**2. push_session.py** (lines 116-120) - Location: `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/push_session.py` - Usage: Reads existing manifest from remote branch for accumulation - File path: `.erk/sessions/manifest.json` - Function: `_read_manifest_from_branch()` (lines 100-126)
**3. fetch_sessions.py** (two calls) - Location: `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/fetch_sessions.py`
a) Lines 44-48 in `_fetch_manifest()` (lines 28-54) - Reads manifest: `.erk/sessions/manifest.json`
b) Lines 77-81 in `_fetch_file_from_branch()` (lines 57-87) - Reads XML files: `.erk/sessions/{filename}`
### Pattern Characteristics
- **Gateway abstraction**: Direct subprocess calls wrapped in a gateway method - **Error handling**: Returns `None` on failure (graceful degradation) - **Usage pattern**: All callers check for `None` and handle absence gracefully - **Operation**: Uses `git show ref:file_path` to read file content without checkout - **Return type**: `bytes | None` - raw bytes when successful, None when file not found
### Related Documentation
The subprocess wrapper pattern is documented in: - `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/architecture/subprocess-wrappers.md` - Explains the two-layer pattern (gateway vs CLI layer) and LBYL patterns for graceful degradation
